posts.raw_content:
Why should Asians and Indians be allowed to take high-paying jobs in technology, engineering, medicine, and academia from the children of the Americans who actually built this country? They have no ancestral claim, no historical stake, and no moral title to the wealth and opportunity that exists here. Their presence in these positions is not the result of any founding compact or shared sacrifice. It is the result of later democrat policy choices, loopholes, and a refusal to enforce the original understanding of who this nation was created for.

Chinese laborers were brought to build the railroads as temporary contract workers. Congress responded with the Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882, which banned the Chinese from settling permanently. It recognized they were never meant to remain or gain citizenship. That law was correct. They stayed anyway because the country at the time lacked the will or capacity for mass removal. The same logic applies to every subsequent Asian group and to Indians. If the Chinese were never intended to settle permanently, then Japanese, Koreans, Vietnamese, Indians, and the rest have no stronger claim. They did not fight the wars of independence. They did not conquer or settle the territory. They never founded the cities or owned the soil. Their descendants, even after several generations, remain culturally and genetically distinct from the European peoples who created the United States.

Today's American children must compete against the populations of entire Asian countries for tech and upper level jobs in their own hometowns. These are six-figure positions in fields that Asians and Indians are systematically occupying. They arrive through targeted immigration channels designed to import talent that displaces Americans. They are extracting wealth and opportunity from a country that was never intended for them. High average intelligence and superficial politeness does not create a right to what belongs to the descendants of those who built everything worth having.

Citizenship obtained through birthright interpretations, chain migration, or later policy changes is paper citizenship. It does not override the fundamental reality that these groups were never part of the American founding project. A nation is not obligated to grant permanent membership and its highest rewards to people whose entry was never meant to be permanent. The Chinese were meant to be temporary contractors: when you hire someone as a contractor to work on your house for a specific project, you do not owe that person and his entire family room, board, and inheritance rights for life.

If the United States is serious about preserving its character as a nation rooted in European legal traditions and the blood and effort of the people who created it, then the current situation cannot continue. Citizenship must be stripped wherever possible. Birthright citizenship must be ended and applied retroactively to those whose presence stems from the original policy error. Asians and Indians must be removed and repatriated to their ancestral homelands. Only those with genuine ties to the historic American people should retain a permanent place. This is the consistent application of history and the recognition that some groups were never meant to become part of the American story or to inherit its rewards at the expense of its children.
